behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on the real LLM's ability to extract and respond to specific context from the email thread. A mocked LLM could be configured to always return context-aware text, hiding the failure mode where the actual model produces generic boilerplate. The test must call the real LLM with real email content to detect when the model fails to maintain specificity.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real email thread with specific details (person names, dates, quoted questions, mentioned projects) to the system. Invoke the actual LLM to generate a draft reply. Verify the draft contains references to at least 3 specific points from the email (e.g., mentions Bob's deadline, references the Q2 project, answers Sarah's specific question) rather than generic phrases.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: If claim extraction from the draft relies on an LLM (common in modern systems), the test requires real LLM inference to extract claims accurately and detect contradictions. A rule-based extraction system would allow Integration-level testing, but LLM-based extraction is the likely implementation. The failure mode—a real LLM-extracted claim contradicting real KB data that the system fails to flag—requires real inference to catch.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft containing a claim that directly contradicts an entry in the knowledge base (e.g., 'We ship to Canada' when KB says 'Canada not supported'). Run the consistency check with the real LLM or rule-based extractor and real KB. Verify that the contradiction is flagged and surfaced to the user with details about which claim contradicted which KB entry.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: This is a prompt-adherence and model behavior test. A mocked LLM can always be configured to include a disclaimer, but the real LLM must be prompted correctly to generate one and must actually follow the instruction under knowledge scarcity. The failure mode is: the real model generates unsourced claims even when KB is empty. Only a real LLM call will reveal this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a knowledge base with no entries relevant to the email thread's topic. Invoke the real LLM to generate a draft reply. Verify the draft includes a clear disclaimer statement (e.g., 'Note: I don't have information about this topic in our knowledge base, so please verify this independently') and contains no factual claims presented without a source.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic application logic that can be tested with mocked I/O. The failure mode—sending a draft without approval—is not dependent on real LLM behavior, real databases, or external systems. A unit test with a mocked email sender can reliably verify that the approval gate is enforced. However, Integration testing is valuable to ensure the approval state threads correctly through the system.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a draft in memory. Attempt to call the send function without setting an approval flag. Verify that the email sender is not invoked (mock assertion). Then set approval to true and call send again. Verify the sender is now invoked exactly once with the correct draft content.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: This behavior spans multiple agentic steps in sequence: real LLM call for draft generation and potentially real LLM/model-based claim extraction for fact-checking. A failure could occur at any step or in the state transitions between them. Testing one agentic component (e.g., just draft generation) would miss failures in later steps or in how results flow between stages. The full pipeline requires Workflow-level testing to verify wiring and sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a real email thread file with varied content. Run the full pipeline: (1) read and parse the thread, (2) invoke real LLM to generate a draft, (3) run fact-check against real KB with real claim extraction, (4) present approval prompt to mock user. Verify: draft is generated and contains expected context, fact-check completes without error, approval prompt is shown with draft and any flags, and the system awaits user approval before exiting.
